sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 11, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Arrow keys move the green square with the WHITE center pixel by 3 cells. MOUSE swaps center colors between the two green squares. Goal is to place each green square into a dark outline. Right green (active, W center) needs to go to right outline at (26-30, 44-48). Left green needs to go to left outline at (32-36, 11-15).
- Plan: UP + RIGHT×5 to place right green, then MOUSE swap, then DOWN + LEFT×2 to place left green.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 10 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The batch only executed UP (1 action) and stopped. The result shows "UP" as the action display, meaning only the first action was executed. Looking at the state, nothing seems to have changed - the green squares are still at the same positions. This is because UP was a "no_impact" action - the active green square (with W center) is the right one at (30-32, 30-32), and UP would move it to (27-29, 30-32). But wait, looking at the output, the green squares are still at the same positions. Let me check...
Actually wait, looking at the output more carefully:
- N px=8 bound=[[30, 30], [30, 32], [32, 32], [32, 30]] - this is the right green square, still at row 30-32
- W px=1 bound=[[31, 31]] - white center still at (31, 31)
So UP didn't move the right green square? But earlier UP DID move the left green square when it had the white center. Let me reconsider...
Wait, the result says "Result: UP" and "Done: False". It only executed 1 action. Maybe the batch was stopped because the first action was no_impact? But the result doesn't show stop_reason.
Actually, looking at the state again - the right green square is at (30-32, 30-32). If UP moves it up by 3, it would go to (27-29, 30-32). But the output shows it's still at (30-32, 30-32). So UP didn't move it.
Hmm, but earlier when the left green had the W center, UP moved it. Now the right green has W center but UP doesn't move it. Maybe UP only works for the left green square regardless of which one has the white center?
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the mechanics are:
- UP/DOWN move the LEFT green square (regardless of color)
- LEFT/RIGHT move the RIGHT green square (regardless of color)
- MOUSE swaps colors
But that contradicts my earlier observation where UP moved the left green when it had W center but didn't move it when it had c center. Unless... the color swap changed something else.
Actually wait, let me re-examine. After the MOUSE swaps:
- Step 4: left center = c, center center = W
- Step 5: UP was called but nothing moved
But earlier:
- Initial: left center = W, center center = B
- UP was called and left green moved up
So it seems like UP only moves the green square that has the WHITE center. When left had W, it moved. When center had W, UP didn't move anything (because the center green is not the left one, and UP only moves the left green square vertically?).
Wait, that's contradictory. Let me think again...
Maybe UP/DOWN only move the LEFT green square, and LEFT/RIGHT only move the RIGHT green square. The color determines which one is "active" for movement. If the left green has W center, UP/DOWN work on it. If the right green has W center, LEFT/RIGHT work on it.
So:
- Left green with W center → UP/DOWN move it
- Right green with W center → LEFT/RIGHT move it
This would explain:
1. Initial: left has W, UP moves left green ✓
2. After swap: right has W, UP doesn't move anything (because UP controls left green, but left green doesn't have W) ✓
3. After swap: right has W, RIGHT moves right green ✓
So to move the right green UP, I need the right green to have W center AND use... but UP only controls the left green? That doesn't make sense for getting the right green to the upper-right target.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe:
- The active square (with W center) responds to ALL arrow keys
- When left green has W, UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT all move the left green
- When right green has W, UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT all move the right green
But the evidence shows:
- Left green with W: UP moved it ✓
- Right green with W: RIGHT moved it ✓
- Right green with W: UP did NOT move it ✗
Unless UP hit a boundary and couldn't move. The right green is at row 30-32. The purple bar is at some position. Maybe there's a wall blocking UP movement for the right green?
Looking at the board, there's a purple vertical bar between the two panels. The right green is to the right of the purple bar. Maybe the right green can only move horizontally (LEFT/RIGHT) because it's on a horizontal track, while the left green can only move vertically (UP/DOWN) because it's on a vertical track?
Wait, but RIGHT moved the right green when it had W center. And earlier, when left green had W center, RIGHT moved ALL objects. So the behavior is different depending on which green has W center.
Let me reconsider more carefully:
- When LEFT green has W center: arrow keys affect the LEFT green square
- When RIGHT green has W center: arrow keys affect the RIGHT green square
But then why did UP not move the right green? Maybe the right green can only move horizontally (LEFT/RIGHT) because it's constrained by the track?
Actually, looking at the board layout:
- Left panel (light gray): contains left dark outline and left green square
- Middle: purple vertical bar and horizontal track
- Right panel (light gray): contains right dark outline
Maybe the left green moves in the left panel (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT within that panel), and the right green moves in the right panel (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT within that panel). But the panels are separated by the purple bar.
Hmm, let me just try RIGHT multiple times to move the right green to the right outline, since I know RIGHT works for the right green with W center.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT green has W center and responds to RIGHT. UP didn't move it, possibly because it's constrained to horizontal movement or there's a boundary. Let me focus on moving the right green rightward first.
Plan: Execute RIGHT×5 to move right green to the right dark outline, then swap and handle left green.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Right green (W center) is at (30-32, 30-32), need to reach (26-30, 44-48)
# Try RIGHT 5 times to move it right
actions = ['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT']
result = action(actions)
print("Executed:", result.get('action_display'))
print("Done:", result.get('done'))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nObjects after RIGHT x5:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
